title: 🌊 Cumbre Vieja: The Atlantic Megatsunami That Could Erase NYC
description: In 2001, geologists Steven Ward and Simon Day published a paper claiming the western flank of Cumbre Vieja volcano on La Palma could collapse and trigger a 50-meter wave that would cross the Atlantic. The 2021 eruption proved the volcano is alive. Most scientists now reject the worst-case model. But the underlying geology is real, the fault scarp is real, and the debate has not gone away. #CumbreVieja #Megatsunami #LaPalma #Volcano #DisasterDecode

segments.voice: On the western flank of La Palma, in the Canary Islands, sits a volcanic ridge called Cumbre Vieja. It is one of the most studied volcanoes on Earth, and not because of its eruptions. In 2001, two geologists published a paper suggesting that a future eruption could detach a section of the western flank — roughly 500 cubic kilometers of rock — and send it sliding into the Atlantic. The resulting wave, they argued, could reach 50 meters along the eastern coast of North America. That single paper changed how millions of people think about volcanic hazard.
segments.visual: Cumbre Vieja volcano La Palma Canary Islands western flank Atlantic ocean ridge

segments.voice: The authors were Steven Ward of the University of California, Santa Cruz, and Simon Day, then at University College London. Their model assumed the entire unstable flank would fail in a single rapid event. Most volcanologists now consider that assumption extreme. Flank collapses do happen on ocean island volcanoes — the seafloor around the Canaries, Hawaii, and Reunion is littered with the debris of past slides. But these events tend to occur in stages, over hours or days, not as a single block dropping into the sea.
segments.visual: Steven Ward Simon Day paper 2001 model flank collapse seafloor debris

segments.voice: Here's what nobody disputes: in 1949, an eruption at Cumbre Vieja opened a fissure system on the western flank. A surface fault scarp formed. The western side of the ridge dropped relative to the eastern side by about four meters in some places. That rupture is still visible today as a line across the volcano. Whatever stress was relieved in 1949 is part of an ongoing process. The flank is moving. The question scientists argue about is how fast it will fail and what kind of slide will result.
segments.visual: 1949 eruption fissure fault scarp western flank movement geological feature

segments.voice: The ridge itself is the most active volcanic structure in the Canaries. Cumbre Vieja has erupted at least seven times since 1500, including 1585, 1646, 1677, 1712, 1949, 1971, and 2021. Each eruption adds new material to the western flank, increasing the gravitational stress on rock that is already weakened by hot fluids and unstable saturated layers below. The volcano grows from the inside out — and as it grows, the flank steepens.
segments.visual: Cumbre Vieja eruption history list 1585 1646 1949 1971 2021 timeline

segments.voice: On September 19, 2021, Cumbre Vieja began erupting again after fifty years of quiet. The Tajogaite eruption lasted 85 days. Lava flows destroyed more than 1,300 homes, buried banana plantations, and added new land to the western coastline. Scientists from Spain's Instituto Geográfico Nacional and the Spanish National Research Council monitored the event in real time. They tracked thousands of earthquakes, ground deformation across the ridge, and the daily growth of the cone above the village of Las Manchas.
segments.visual: Tajogaite eruption 2021 lava flow homes destroyed Las Manchas cone

segments.voice: Despite intense public attention to the megatsunami hypothesis, the 2021 eruption produced no significant flank movement. GPS stations across the western ridge measured displacement in centimeters, not meters. The flank deformed slightly, then settled. This was strong evidence that a single catastrophic collapse is not the most likely outcome of an eruption. But it did not eliminate the hazard. The flank is still there. The fault scarp is still there. And eruptions will keep coming.
segments.visual: GPS stations centimeter displacement deformation western flank monitoring data

segments.voice: To understand the megatsunami debate, you need the original numbers. Ward and Day estimated a slide volume of 150 to 500 cubic kilometers. Their initial wave at the source: 600 to 900 meters tall. After 250 kilometers of travel: 50 to 100 meters. Reaching the Caribbean: 10 to 25 meters. Reaching the eastern United States: 20 to 25 meters along the coastline. They modeled this with the assumption of a single coherent slide moving at about 100 meters per second.
segments.visual: Ward Day model wave heights numbers 600 meters 50 meters Atlantic propagation

segments.voice: The criticisms came quickly. In 2008, geologists led by Raphaël Paris and others argued that the modeled slide volume was too large and the failure mechanism was unrealistic. Studies of past slides on the Canary seafloor showed that they happen in multiple smaller pulses. Each pulse generates a wave, but the waves do not add coherently — they interfere, they spread, they lose energy. The implied wave heights for the eastern United States dropped to one or two meters. Significant for low-lying coasts, but not civilization-ending.
segments.visual: Raphaël Paris 2008 critique multiple pulses smaller waves seafloor evidence

segments.voice: Then there is the question of distance. The eastern United States lies more than 5,500 kilometers from La Palma. Even in the worst-case Ward-Day scenario, the wave would take about seven hours to arrive. That is enough time, in theory, to evacuate coastal cities — if anyone believed the warning. The bigger immediate impact would fall on the Canary Islands themselves, on Western Sahara, and on the closer islands of Madeira and the Azores. Those coastlines could see waves of tens of meters within minutes.
segments.visual: tsunami travel time map Atlantic 7 hours coastal evacuation distance

segments.voice: But the tremor was just the start of the story. Spain's National Geographic Institute, working with the Volcanological Institute of the Canaries, has installed one of the densest geophysical networks in the world on La Palma. Permanent GPS stations. Tiltmeters embedded in the ground. Strain meters inside boreholes. Satellite radar measurements. Seismometers tracking magma movement underground. The goal is to detect any signal that flank movement has shifted from slow creep to accelerated motion — a precursor of a real failure.
segments.visual: monitoring network GPS tiltmeters seismometers satellite radar La Palma instruments

segments.voice: The deeper question is what kind of slide is even possible. Computer modelers in 2018 used high-resolution sonar maps of the seafloor west of La Palma. They found terraces, scarps, and debris fans consistent with multiple slides over hundreds of thousands of years. The largest individual debris field, the Cumbre Nueva slide, contains roughly 95 cubic kilometers of material. That is far smaller than Ward and Day's worst case. It also occurred about 560,000 years ago, before any modern coastal city existed.
segments.visual: sonar seafloor map terraces debris fans Cumbre Nueva slide ancient deposits

segments.voice: What does this geology say about the future? Slides will happen again. The volcanic process that built the islands will eventually unbuild parts of them. But the timescales are measured in tens to hundreds of thousands of years. The probability of a major flank collapse in any given century is low. The probability of a megatsunami matching Ward-Day's model in any given lifetime is much lower than the probability of an earthquake-driven Pacific tsunami like Tohoku 2011.
segments.visual: geological timescale slide probability comparison Pacific Atlantic risk

segments.voice: Tohoku itself reset what scientists thought tsunamis could do. On March 11, 2011, a magnitude 9.1 earthquake off Japan's coast generated a wave that overtopped seawalls designed for the worst recorded historical events. Survey teams from the Japan Meteorological Agency and Tohoku University recorded run-up heights above 39 meters along the Sanriku coast. Tohoku showed that historical record is not always a guide to maximum hazard. That lesson now informs every tsunami study, including Cumbre Vieja's.
segments.visual: Tohoku 2011 wave overtops seawall 39 meter run-up Sanriku coast Japan

segments.voice: Within the volcanological community, there is broad consensus on a few points. The flank is moving. Eruptions will keep destabilizing it. Future slides are inevitable on geological timescales. A single large coherent slide is unlikely. Smaller multi-stage failures are more probable. Tsunamis from such events would be regionally devastating but not transatlantic in the Ward-Day sense. The remaining uncertainty is large enough that further monitoring and modeling are essential, not optional.
segments.visual: scientific consensus points flank moving slides inevitable smaller events regional

segments.voice: What makes Cumbre Vieja different from a typical volcano is the combination of factors. It sits on a young oceanic island. It builds quickly. It contains hot intrusions that weaken the rock. It has a known active fault on its flank. It is surrounded by deep ocean. Any rock that detaches has nowhere to go but the seafloor — and any slide of significant volume into that ocean will generate a wave. Most of those waves will be small. Some, on long timescales, will be large.
segments.visual: Cumbre Vieja unique factors young island fault deep ocean hazard combination

segments.voice: Compare it to Hawaii's Big Island. The Hilina slump on Kīlauea's south flank is moving at about 10 centimeters per year. It generated tsunami in the 1868 Hawaiian earthquake. Geologists tracking it know it will fail eventually. The Nuʻuanu slide off Oʻahu, dated to about 2.4 million years ago, displaced more than 5,000 cubic kilometers — roughly ten times the entire Cumbre Vieja flank. Its tsunami left deposits 300 meters above sea level on Lanai. Ocean island flank collapses are real. They have happened before.
segments.visual: Hilina slump Kīlauea Nuʻuanu slide Lanai deposits 300 meters Hawaiian comparison

segments.voice: The Storegga slide off Norway around 8,200 years ago is another reference event. About 3,500 cubic kilometers of seafloor sediment failed. The resulting tsunami struck Scotland with run-up of 25 meters in places. Stone Age coastal settlements were swept away. Storegga was not a volcanic flank — it was a submarine landslide triggered by methane hydrate dissociation and earthquake shaking. But it demonstrates the kind of multi-thousand-cubic-kilometer slide that can happen, and the kind of waves that result.
segments.visual: Storegga slide Norway 8200 years Scotland 25 meter run-up Stone Age tsunami

segments.voice: For the Cumbre Vieja hazard, the practical questions are about local response, not New York City evacuation. If a slide begins, how much warning will instruments give? Minutes? Hours? Where will people on La Palma's western coast go? The high road across the island? Boats out to sea? The Canary Islands government has run drills and updated evacuation plans, especially after 2021. Tourist hotels along the coast carry tsunami signage. The hazard is treated as real, regardless of which model is right.
segments.visual: evacuation plan La Palma western coast warning signs drills tourist response

segments.voice: Across the Atlantic, the United States runs the National Tsunami Warning Center in Palmer, Alaska, and a Pacific-Atlantic monitoring grid that includes the DART buoy network. DART buoys measure pressure changes on the seafloor and transmit real-time data via satellite. A wave from La Palma would cross multiple DART arrays before reaching the eastern seaboard. Detection in deep water is high-confidence. Forecasting near-coast run-up is the harder problem, because shoreline shape amplifies waves unpredictably.
segments.visual: DART buoy network seafloor pressure satellite real-time monitoring tsunami warning

segments.voice: Public communication around Cumbre Vieja has often diverged sharply from the science. Sensational documentaries and online videos have presented the Ward-Day worst case as imminent and certain. That treatment has produced both real fear and real fatigue. When the 2021 eruption began, social media filled with predictions of an imminent transatlantic wave. Spanish authorities, NOAA, and the United States Geological Survey issued statements emphasizing that no such event was occurring or expected.
segments.visual: social media misinformation 2021 eruption viral predictions agency statements

segments.voice: Here's what the data actually showed during the 2021 eruption. The Tajogaite cone produced about 220 million cubic meters of lava. Compared to the slide volumes Ward-Day modeled, that is roughly 0.15 percent. Ground deformation maxed out at a few centimeters of horizontal motion in the most active areas. Seismicity was dominated by short-period magmatic events, not by the long-period signals associated with large bulk rock movement. The volcano did exactly what most monitoring scientists expected.
segments.visual: Tajogaite lava volume 220 million cubic meters minor ground deformation seismicity

segments.voice: Cumbre Vieja sits inside a broader debate about how to communicate low-probability, high-consequence hazards. Earthquakes on the New Madrid fault, eruptions of Yellowstone, slips on the Cascadia subduction zone — all share the property that they are extremely unlikely in any given year but devastating when they occur. The Cumbre Vieja megatsunami case is the same shape. The honest answer is rarely simple. The answer is a probability curve, not a yes or no.
segments.visual: low probability high consequence New Madrid Yellowstone Cascadia probability curve

segments.voice: What scientists working on La Palma actually do day to day is model continuously. The 2018 EU-funded project ASTARTE produced revised tsunami scenarios for the Atlantic, treating Cumbre Vieja as one of dozens of source possibilities. Spain's CSIC research arm runs annual updates of flank kinematics. Universities in the United Kingdom, Germany, and the United States continue to publish on the slide problem. The work is incremental. Each year the uncertainty narrows by a small amount.
segments.visual: ASTARTE project 2018 flank kinematics CSIC research papers scientific work

segments.voice: If a future eruption did show signs of accelerating flank motion — meters per day instead of millimeters per year — that would be the warning sign. Detection systems are tuned to pick up that kind of departure from baseline. There is no published case, anywhere in the world, of an ocean island flank failing without precursor signals. Hawaii, Réunion, Stromboli, and the Canaries have all shown elevated seismicity, deformation, or temperature changes ahead of major collapses.
segments.visual: precursor signs accelerated motion meters per day baseline departure warning

segments.voice: Subscribe to DisasterDecode and hit the bell. Next week's disaster goes deeper into Yellowstone — the supervolcano, the magma chamber, and what fifty years of monitoring data actually show about a future eruption.
segments.visual: Yellowstone supervolcano magma chamber monitoring channel subscription bell
